title: Personal name

infobox.value: or **prosoponym** (from [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") *prósōpon* – person, and *onoma* –name)[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeats-Rohan2007164-165-1) is the set of [names](/wiki/Name "Name") by which an individual person or animal is known. When taken together as a [phrase](/wiki/Phrase "Phrase"), they all relate to that one individual.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoom199679-2) In many cultures, the term is synonymous with the *[birth name](/wiki/Birth_name "Birth name")* or *[legal name](/wiki/Legal_name "Legal name")* of the individual. In [linguistic](/wiki/Linguistic "Linguistic") classification, personal names are studied within a specific [onomastic](/wiki/Onomastic "Onomastic") discipline, called [anthroponymy](/wiki/Anthroponymy "Anthroponymy").[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoom19968-3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/FML_names-2.png/330px-FML_names-2.png)](/wiki/File:FML_names-2.png)

First/given, middle and last/family/surname with [John Fitzgerald Kennedy](/wiki/John_Fitzgerald_Kennedy "John Fitzgerald Kennedy") as example. This shows a structure typical for the [Anglosphere](/wiki/Anglosphere "Anglosphere"), among others. Other cultures use other structures for full names.

A **personal name**, **full name** or **prosoponym** (from [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") *prósōpon* – person, and *onoma* –name)[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeats-Rohan2007164-165-1) is the set of [names](/wiki/Name "Name") by which an individual person or animal is known. When taken together as a [phrase](/wiki/Phrase "Phrase"), they all relate to that one individual.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoom199679-2) In many cultures, the term is synonymous with the *[birth name](/wiki/Birth_name "Birth name")* or *[legal name](/wiki/Legal_name "Legal name")* of the individual. In [linguistic](/wiki/Linguistic "Linguistic") classification, personal names are studied within a specific [onomastic](/wiki/Onomastic "Onomastic") discipline, called [anthroponymy](/wiki/Anthroponymy "Anthroponymy").[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoom19968-3)

In [Western culture](/wiki/Western_culture "Western culture"), nearly all individuals possess at least one *[given name](/wiki/Given_name "Given name")* (also known as a *first name*, *forename*, or *[Christian name](/wiki/Christian_name "Christian name")*), together with a *[surname](/wiki/Surname "Surname")* (also known as a *last name* or *family name*). In the name "James Smith", for example, *James* is the first name and *Smith* is the surname. Surnames in the West generally indicate that the individual belongs to a family, a tribe, or a clan, although the exact relationships vary: they may be given at birth, taken upon adoption, [changed upon marriage](/wiki/Maiden_and_married_names "Maiden and married names"), and so on. Where there are two or more given names, typically only one (in English-speaking cultures usually the first) is used in normal speech.

Another naming convention that is used mainly in the [Arabic culture](/wiki/Arab_culture "Arab culture") and in different other areas across Africa and Asia is connecting the person's given name with a chain of names, starting with the name of the person's father and then the father's father and so on, usually ending with the family name (tribe or clan name). However, the legal full name of a person usually contains the first three names (given name, father's name, father's father's name) and the family name at the end, to limit the name in government-issued ID. Men's names and women's names are constructed using the same convention, and a person's name is not altered if they are married.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4)

Some cultures, including Western ones, also add (or once added) [patronymics](/wiki/Patronymic "Patronymic") or [matronymics](/wiki/Matronymic "Matronymic"), for instance as a middle name as with [Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky](/wiki/Pyotr_Ilyich_Tchaikovsky "Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky") (whose father's given name was Ilya), or as a last name as with [Björk Guðmundsdóttir](/wiki/Bj%C3%B6rk_Gu%C3%B0mundsd%C3%B3ttir "Björk Guðmundsdóttir") (whose father is named [Guðmundur](/wiki/Gu%C3%B0mundur_Gunnarsson "Guðmundur Gunnarsson")) or [Heiðar Helguson](/wiki/Hei%C3%B0ar_Helguson "Heiðar Helguson") (whose mother was named Helga). Similar concepts are present in [Eastern](/wiki/Eastern_world "Eastern world") cultures. However, in some areas of the world, many people are known by a single name, and so are said to be [mononymous](/wiki/Mononymous_person "Mononymous person"). Still other cultures lack the concept of specific, fixed names designating people, either individually or collectively. Certain isolated tribes, such as the [Machiguenga](/wiki/Machiguenga_people "Machiguenga people") of the Amazon, do not use personal names.[[i]](#cite_note-7)

It is nearly universal for people to have names; the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") [Convention on the Rights of the Child](/wiki/Convention_on_the_Rights_of_the_Child "Convention on the Rights of the Child") declares that a child has the right to a name from birth.[[7]](#cite_note-8)

## Structure in humans

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Personal_name&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Structure in humans")]

Common components of names given at birth can include:

* Personal name: The [given name](/wiki/Given_name "Given name") (or acquired name in some cultures) can precede a [family name](/wiki/Family_name "Family name") (as in most European cultures), or it can come after the family name (as in some East Asian cultures and Hungary), or be used without a family name.
* [Patronymic](/wiki/Patronymic "Patronymic"): A surname based on the given name of the father.
* [Matronymic](/wiki/Matronymic "Matronymic"): A surname based on the given name of the mother.
* [Family name](/wiki/Family_name "Family name"): A name used by all members of a family.

In China, surnames gradually came into common use beginning in the 3rd century BC (having been common only among the nobility before that). In some areas of East Asia (e.g. Korea and Vietnam), surnames developed in the next several centuries, while in other areas (like Japan), surnames did not become prevalent until the 19th century. In Europe, after the loss of the [Roman system](/wiki/Roman_naming_conventions "Roman naming conventions"), the common use of family names started quite early in some areas (France in the 13th century, and Germany in the 16th century), but it often did not happen until much later in areas that used a patronymic naming custom, such as the [Scandinavian](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia") countries, Wales, and some areas of Germany, as well as Russia and Ukraine. The compulsory use of surnames varied greatly. France required a priest to write surnames in baptismal records in 1539 (but did not require surnames for Jews, who usually used [patronymics](/wiki/Patronymic "Patronymic"), until 1808). On the other hand, surnames were not compulsory in the Scandinavian countries until the 19th or 20th century (1923 in Norway), and Iceland still [does not use surnames](/wiki/Icelandic_name "Icelandic name") for its native inhabitants. In most of the cultures of the Middle East and South Asia, surnames were not generally used until European influence took hold in the 19th century.

In Spain and most Latin American countries, two surnames are used, one being the father's family name and the other being the mother's family name. In Spain, the second surname is sometimes informally used alone if the first one is too common to allow an easy identification. For example, former Spanish Prime Minister [José Luis Rodríguez Zapatero](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Luis_Rodr%C3%ADguez_Zapatero "José Luis Rodríguez Zapatero") is often called just *Zapatero*. In Argentina, only the father's last name is used, in most cases.

In most Portuguese-speaking countries typically [two surnames are used](/wiki/Portuguese_name "Portuguese name"), sometimes three or four, typically some or none inherited from the mother and some or all inherited from the father, in that order. Co-parental siblings most often share an identical string of surnames. For collation, shortening, and formal addressing, the last of these surnames is typically preferred. A Portuguese person named António Manuel de Oliveira Guterres would therefore be known commonly as [António Guterres](/wiki/Ant%C3%B3nio_Guterres "António Guterres").

In [Russia](/wiki/Eastern_Slavic_naming_customs "Eastern Slavic naming customs"), the first name and family name conform to the usual Western practice, but the middle name is [patronymic](/wiki/Patronymic "Patronymic").[[8]](#cite_note-9) Thus, all the children of Ivan Volkov would be named "[first name] Ivanovich Volkov" if male, or "[first name] Ivanovna Volkova" if female (-ovich meaning "son of", -ovna meaning "daughter of",[[9]](#cite_note-10) and -a usually being appended to the surnames of girls). However, in formal Russian name order, the surname comes first, followed by the given name and patronymic, such as "Raskolnikov Rodion Romanovich".[[10]](#cite_note-11)

In many families, single or multiple [middle names](/wiki/Middle_name "Middle name") are simply alternative names, names honoring an ancestor or relative, or, for married women, sometimes their maiden names. In some traditions, however, the roles of the first and middle given names are reversed, with the first given name being used to honor a family member and the middle name being used as the usual method to address someone informally. Many [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") families choose a [saint](/wiki/Saint "Saint")'s name as their child's middle name or this can be left until the child's [confirmation](/wiki/Confirmation_(sacrament) "Confirmation (sacrament)") when they choose a saint's name for themselves. Cultures that use [patronymics](/wiki/Patronymic "Patronymic") or [matronymics](/wiki/Matronymic "Matronymic") will often give middle names to distinguish between two similarly named people: e.g., Einar Karl Stefánsson and Einar Guðmundur Stefánsson. This is especially done in Iceland (as shown in example) where people are known and referred to almost exclusively by their given name/s.

Some people (called *anonyms*) choose to be [anonymous](/wiki/Anonymity "Anonymity"), that is, to hide their true names, for fear of governmental prosecution or social ridicule of their works or actions. Another method to disguise one's identity is to employ a [pseudonym](/wiki/Pseudonym "Pseudonym").

For some people, their name is a single word, known as a [mononym](/wiki/Mononym "Mononym"). This can be true from birth, or occur later in life. For example, [Teller](/wiki/Teller_(entertainer) "Teller (entertainer)"), of the magician duo [Penn and Teller](/wiki/Penn_and_Teller "Penn and Teller"), was named Raymond Joseph Teller at birth, but changed his name both legally and socially to be simply "Teller". In some official government documents, such as his [driver's license](/wiki/Driver%27s_license "Driver's license"), his given name is listed as *NFN*, an initialism for "no first name".

The [Inuit](/wiki/Inuit "Inuit") believe that the [souls](/wiki/Soul_(spirit) "Soul (spirit)") of the [namesakes](/wiki/Namesake "Namesake") are one, so they traditionally refer to the junior namesakes, not just by the names (*atiq*), but also by [kinship](/wiki/Kinship "Kinship") title, which applies across gender and generation without implications of disrespect or seniority.

In [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism"), someone's name is considered intimately connected with their fate, and adding a name (e.g. on the sickbed) may avert a particular danger. Among [Ashkenazi Jews](/wiki/Ashkenazi_Jews "Ashkenazi Jews"), it is also considered bad luck to take the name of a living ancestor, as the [Angel of Death](/wiki/Personifications_of_death#In_Judaism "Personifications of death") may mistake the younger person for their namesake (although there is no such custom among [Sephardi Jews](/wiki/Sephardi_Jews "Sephardi Jews")). Jews may also have a Jewish name for intra-community use and use a different name when engaging with the [Gentile](/wiki/Goyim "Goyim") world.

[Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_emperor "Chinese emperor") and [Japanese emperors](/wiki/Japanese_emperor "Japanese emperor") receive [posthumous names](/wiki/Posthumous_name "Posthumous name").

In some [Polynesian cultures](/wiki/Polynesian_culture "Polynesian culture"), the name of a deceased chief becomes [taboo](/wiki/Taboo "Taboo"). If he is named after a common object or concept, a different word has to be used for it.

In [Cameroon](/wiki/Cameroon "Cameroon"), there is "a great deal of mobility" within naming structure. Some Cameroonians, particularly Anglophone Cameroonians, use "a characteristic sequencing" starting with a first surname, followed by a forename then a second surname (e.g. Awanto Josephine Nchang), while others begin with a forename followed by first and then second surnames (e.g. Josephine Awanto Nchang). The latter structure is rare in Francophone Cameroon, however, where a third structure prevails: First surname, second surname, forename (e.g. Awanto Nchang Josephine).[[11]](#cite_note-cameroon-12)

Depending on national convention, additional given names (and sometimes [titles](/wiki/Title "Title")) are considered part of the name.

## Legal names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Personal_name&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Legal names")]

A person's personal name is usually their full [legal name](/wiki/Legal_name "Legal name"); however, some people use only part of their full legal name, a [title](/wiki/Title "Title"), [nickname](/wiki/Nickname "Nickname"), [pseudonym](/wiki/Pseudonym "Pseudonym") or other chosen name that is different from their legal name, and reserve their legal name for legal and administrative purposes.[[12]](#cite_note-13) Since October 2025, [Laurence Watkins](/wiki/Laurence_Watkins "Laurence Watkins") has held the record for the longest legal name, with 2,253 names as part of his full name.[[13]](#cite_note-14)[[14]](#cite_note-15)

The [royalty](/wiki/Royal_family "Royal family"), [nobility](/wiki/Nobility "Nobility"), and [gentry](/wiki/Gentry "Gentry") of Europe traditionally have many names, including phrases for the lands that they own. The French developed the method of putting the term by which the person is referred in [small capital](/wiki/Small_capital "Small capital") letters. It is this habit which transferred to names of Eastern Asia, as seen below. An example is that of Marie-Joseph-Paul-Yves-Roch Gilbert du Motier, who is known as the [Marquis de La Fayette](/wiki/Gilbert_du_Motier,_marquis_de_La_Fayette "Gilbert du Motier, marquis de La Fayette"). He possessed both the lands of Motier and La Fayette.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The bare place name was used formerly to refer to the person who owned it, rather than the land itself (the word "Gloucester" in "What will Gloucester do?" meant the [Duke of Gloucester](/wiki/Duke_of_Gloucester "Duke of Gloucester")). As a development, the bare name of a ship in the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") meant its captain (e.g., "Cressy didn't learn from Aboukir") while the name with an article referred to the ship (e.g., "The *Cressy* is foundering").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Naming conventions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Personal_name&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Naming conventions")]

A personal naming system, or anthroponymic system, is a system describing the choice of personal name in a certain society. Personal names consist of one or more parts, such as [given name](/wiki/Given_name "Given name"), [surname](/wiki/Surname "Surname") and [patronymic](/wiki/Patronymic "Patronymic"). Personal naming systems are studied within the field of [anthroponymy](/wiki/Anthroponymy "Anthroponymy").

In contemporary Western societies (except for Iceland, Hungary, and sometimes [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders"), depending on the occasion), the most common naming convention is that a person must have a [given name](/wiki/Given_name "Given name"), which is usually gender-specific, followed by the parents' [family name](/wiki/Family_name "Family name"). In [onomastic](/wiki/Onomastic "Onomastic") terminology, given names of male persons are called *andronyms* (from [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") ἀνήρ / man, and ὄνομα / name),[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoom19966-16) while given names of female persons are called *gynonyms* (from [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") γυνή / woman, and ὄνομα / name).[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarolini200591,_98-17)

Some given names are bespoke, but most are repeated from earlier generations in the same culture. Many are drawn from mythology, some of which span multiple language areas. This has resulted in related names in different languages (e.g. [George](/wiki/George_(given_name) "George (given name)"), [Georg](/wiki/Georg_(disambiguation) "Georg (disambiguation)"), [Jorge](/wiki/Jorge "Jorge")), which might be translated or might be maintained as immutable proper nouns.

In earlier times, Scandinavian countries followed patronymic naming, with people effectively called "X's son/daughter"; this is now the case only in Iceland and was recently re-introduced as an option in the [Faroe Islands](/wiki/Faroe_Islands "Faroe Islands"). It is legally possible in Finland as people of Icelandic ethnic naming are specifically named in the name law. When people of this name convert to standards of other cultures, the phrase is often condensed into one word, creating last names like Jacobsen (Jacob's Son).

There is a range of personal naming systems:[[17]](#cite_note-18)

* Binomial systems: apart from their given name, people are described by their surnames, which they obtain from one of their parents. Most modern European personal naming systems are of this type.
* Patronymic systems: apart from their given name, people are described by their [patronymics](/wiki/Patronymic "Patronymic"), that is, given names (not surnames) of parents or other ancestors. Such systems were in wide use throughout Europe in the first millennium CE, but were replaced by binomial systems. The [Icelandic system](/wiki/Icelandic_name "Icelandic name") is still patronymic.
* More complex systems like [Arabic system](/wiki/Arabic_name "Arabic name"), consisting of [paedonymic](/wiki/Paedonymic "Paedonymic") (son's name), given name, patronymic and one or two [bynames](/wiki/Byname "Byname").

Different cultures have different conventions for personal names.

#### Generational designation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Personal_name&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Generational designation")]

When names are repeated across generations, the senior or junior generation (or both) may be designed with the [name suffix](/wiki/Suffix_(name) "Suffix (name)") "Sr." or "Jr.", respectively (in the former case, retrospectively); or, more formally, by an [ordinal](/wiki/Ordinal_numeral "Ordinal numeral") [Roman number](/wiki/Roman_number "Roman number") such as "I", "II" or "III". In the Catholic tradition, papal names are distinguished in sequence, and may be reused many times, such as [Leo XIV](/wiki/Pope_Leo_XIV "Pope Leo XIV") (the 14th pope assuming the papal name "Leo").

In the case of the American presidents [George H. W. Bush](/wiki/George_H._W._Bush "George H. W. Bush") and his son [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush"), distinct middle initials serve this purpose instead, necessitating their more frequent use. The improvised and unofficial "Bush Sr." and "Bush Jr." were nevertheless tossed about in banter on many [entertainment journalism](/wiki/Entertainment_journalism "Entertainment journalism") opinion panels; alternatively, they became distinguished merely as "W." and "H. W.".

#### Rank, title, honour, accreditation, and affiliation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Personal_name&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Rank, title, honour, accreditation, and affiliation")]

In formal address, personal names may be preceded by [pre-nominal letters](/wiki/Pre-nominal_letters "Pre-nominal letters"), giving title (e.g. Dr., [Captain](/wiki/Military_rank "Military rank")), or social rank, which is commonly gendered (e.g. Mr., Mrs., Ms., Miss.) and might additionally convey [marital status](/wiki/Marital_status "Marital status"). Historically, professional titles such as "Doctor" and "Reverend" were largely confined to male professions, so these were implicitly gendered.

In formal address, personal names, inclusive of a generational designation, if any, may be followed by one or more [post-nominal letters](/wiki/Post-nominal_letters "Post-nominal letters") giving office, honour, decoration, accreditation, or formal affiliation.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/MomoiExpoCrop.png/250px-MomoiExpoCrop.png)](/wiki/File:MomoiExpoCrop.png)

The name card of this Japanese voice actress features her name as "[Halko Momoi](/wiki/Halko_Momoi "Halko Momoi")", written in Western name order, whereas her name in Japanese, which uses Eastern order, would be *Momoi Haruko*.

The order *given name(s), family name* is commonly known as the **Western name order** and is usually used in European countries and in non-European countries that have cultures predominantly influenced by Western Europe (e.g. the United States, Canada, Australia, and New Zealand). It is also used in non-Western regions such as [Northern](/wiki/North_India "North India"), [Eastern](/wiki/East_India "East India"), [Central](/wiki/Central_India "Central India") and [Western India](/wiki/Western_India "Western India"); Pakistan; Bangladesh; Nepal, Sri Lanka, Thailand; Saudi Arabia; Singapore; Malaysia (most of, non-traditional); and the Philippines.

Within alphabetic lists and catalogs, however, the family name is generally put first, with the given name(s) following and separated by a [comma](/wiki/Comma "Comma") (e.g. [Jobs, Steve](/wiki/Steve_Jobs "Steve Jobs") or [Van Gerwen, Michael](/wiki/Michael_van_Gerwen "Michael van Gerwen")) representing the "lexical name order". This convention is followed by most Western libraries, as well as on many administrative forms. In some countries, such as France,[[18]](#cite_note-19) or countries previously part of the former Soviet Union, the comma may be dropped and the swapped form of the name be uttered as such, perceived as a mark of bureaucratic formality. In the USSR and now Russia, personal initials are often written in the "family name - given name - patronymic name" order when signing official documents ([Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"): ФИО, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Russian "Romanization of Russian"): *FIO*), e.g. "[Rachmaninoff S.V.](/wiki/Sergei_Rachmaninoff "Sergei Rachmaninoff")".

### Eastern name order (family name at the first)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Personal_name&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Eastern name order (family name at the first)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Hazank_s_a_Kulfold-21_May_1865.jpg/250px-Hazank_s_a_Kulfold-21_May_1865.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hazank_s_a_Kulfold-21_May_1865.jpg)

[Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln")'s name is spelled as "*Lincoln Ábrahám*" in this magazine from Hungary, the only country in Europe that officially uses the Eastern order – even foreign names used to be Hungarianized up until the 20th century.[[19]](#cite_note-20)

The order *family name, given name*, commonly known as the **Eastern name order**, began to be prominently used in [Ancient China](/wiki/Ancient_China "Ancient China")[[20]](#cite_note-21) and subsequently influenced the [East Asian cultural sphere](/wiki/East_Asian_cultural_sphere "East Asian cultural sphere") (China, Taiwan, Japan, Korea, and Vietnam) and particularly among the Chinese communities in Cambodia, Laos, Indonesia, Malaysia, Singapore, or the Philippines. It is also used in the [southern](/wiki/South_India "South India") and [northeastern](/wiki/Northeast_India "Northeast India") parts of India, and by the [Khmer](/wiki/Khmer_people "Khmer people")[[21]](#cite_note-huffman-22)[[22]](#cite_note-short-23) and [Hmong](/wiki/Hmong_people "Hmong people")[[23]](#cite_note-24) of [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia"), as well as by [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary"), and the [Mordvins](/wiki/Mordvins "Mordvins") of the [former Soviet Union](/wiki/Post-Soviet_states "Post-Soviet states"). In Uganda, the ordering "traditional family name first, Western origin given name second" is also frequently used.[[24]](#cite_note-25) Eastern name order may occasionally be used in rural areas of Germany in informal, spoken contexts.

When East Asian names are [transliterated](/wiki/Transliteration "Transliteration") into the [Latin alphabet](/wiki/Latin_alphabet "Latin alphabet"), some people prefer to convert them to the Western order, while others leave them in the Eastern order but write the family name in [capital letters](/wiki/Letter_case "Letter case"). To avoid confusion, there is a convention in some language communities, e.g., French, that the family name should be written in all capitals when engaging in formal correspondence or writing for an international audience. In Hungarian, the Eastern order of Japanese names is officially kept, and Hungarian transliteration is used (e.g. [*Mijazaki Hajao* in Hungarian](https://hu.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mijazaki_Hajao "hu:Mijazaki Hajao")), but Western name order is also sometimes used with English transliteration (e.g. [Hayao Miyazaki](/wiki/Hayao_Miyazaki "Hayao Miyazaki")). This is also true for Hungarian names in Japanese, e.g. [Ferenc Puskás](/wiki/Ferenc_Pusk%C3%A1s "Ferenc Puskás") (Japanese: プシュカーシュ・フェレンツ, written in the same Eastern order "Puskás Ferenc").

Starting from the [Meiji Restoration](/wiki/Meiji_Restoration "Meiji Restoration") in 1868, the Western name order was primarily used among the Japanese nobility when identifying themselves to non-Asians with their [romanized](/wiki/Romanization "Romanization") names. As a result, in popular Western publications, this order became increasingly used for Japanese names in the subsequent decades.[[25]](#cite_note-Saeki-26) In 2020, the [Government of Japan](/wiki/Government_of_Japan "Government of Japan") reverted the Westernized name order back to the Eastern name order in official documents (e.g. [identity documents](/wiki/Identity_documents "Identity documents"), [academic certificates](/wiki/Academic_certificate "Academic certificate"), [birth certificates](/wiki/Birth_certificates "Birth certificates"), [marriage certificates](/wiki/Marriage_certificate "Marriage certificate"), among others), which means writing family name first in capital letters and has recommended that the same format be used among the general Japanese public.[[26]](#cite_note-JPN-27)

Japan has also requested Western publications to respect this change, such as not using [Shinzo Abe](/wiki/Shinzo_Abe "Shinzo Abe") but rather Abe Shinzo, similar to how Chinese leader [Xi Jinping](/wiki/Xi_Jinping "Xi Jinping") is not referred to as Jinping Xi.[[27]](#cite_note-28) Its sluggish response by Western publications was met with ire by Japanese politician [Taro Kono](/wiki/Taro_Kono "Taro Kono"), who stated that "If you can write [Moon Jae-in](/wiki/Moon_Jae-in "Moon Jae-in") and [Xi Jinping](/wiki/Xi_Jinping "Xi Jinping") in correct order, you can surely write Abe Shinzo the same way."[[28]](#cite_note-29)

#### East Asia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Personal_name&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: East Asia")]

Chinese, Koreans, and other East Asian peoples, except for those traveling or living outside of China and areas influenced by China, rarely reverse their Chinese and Korean language names to the Western naming order. Western publications also preserve this Eastern naming order for Chinese, Korean and other East Asian individuals, with the family name first, followed by the given name.[[29]](#cite_note-30)

[Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese") names of [Hong Kong people](/wiki/Hongkongers "Hongkongers") are usually written in the Eastern order with or without a comma (e.g. Bai Chiu En or Bai, Chiu En). Outside Hong Kong, they are usually written in Western order. Unlike other East Asian countries, the syllables or [logograms](/wiki/Logogram "Logogram") of given names are not hyphenated or compounded but instead separated by a space (e.g. Chiu En). Outside East Asia, the second syllables are often confused with middle names regardless of name order. Some computer systems could not handle given name inputs with space characters.

Some Chinese, Malaysians, and Singaporeans may have an anglicised given name, which is always written in the Western order. The English and transliterated Chinese full names can be written in various orders. A hybrid order is preferred in official documents including the [legislative](/wiki/Legislative_Council_(Hong_Kong) "Legislative Council (Hong Kong)") records in the case for Hong Kong.

Examples of the hybrid order goes in the form of Hong Kong actor "[Tony Leung Chiu-wai](/wiki/Tony_Leung_Chiu-wai "Tony Leung Chiu-wai")" or Singapore Prime Minister "[Lawrence Wong Shyun Tsai](/wiki/Lawrence_Wong_Shyun_Tsai "Lawrence Wong Shyun Tsai")", with family names (in the example, Leung and Wong) shared in the middle. Therefore, the anglicised names are written in the Western order (Tony Leung, Lawrence Wong) and the Chinese names are written in the Eastern order (Leung Chiu-wai, 梁朝偉; Wong Shyun Tsai, 黄循财).

##### Japanese

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Personal_name&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Japanese")]

Japanese use the Eastern naming order (family name followed by given name). In contrast to China and Korea, due to familiarity, Japanese names of contemporary people are usually "switched" when people who have such names are mentioned in media in Western countries; for example, *Koizumi Jun'ichirō* is known as [Junichiro Koizumi](/wiki/Junichiro_Koizumi "Junichiro Koizumi") in English. Japan has requested that Western publications cease this practice of placing their names in the Western name order and revert to the Eastern name order.[[26]](#cite_note-JPN-27)

[Mongols](/wiki/Mongols "Mongols") use the Eastern naming order ([patronymic](/wiki/Patronymic "Patronymic") followed by given name), which is also used there when rendering the names of other East Asians. However, Russian and other Western names (with the exception of Hungarian names) are still written in Western order.

#### South India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Personal_name&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: South India")]

##### Telugu

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Personal_name&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Telugu")]

[Telugu people](/wiki/Telugu_people "Telugu people") from [Andhra Pradesh](/wiki/Andhra_Pradesh "Andhra Pradesh") and [Telangana](/wiki/Telangana "Telangana") traditionally use *family name, given name* order.[[30]](#cite_note-31) The family name first format is different from [North India](/wiki/North_India "North India") where family name typically appears last or other parts of South India where [patronymic](/wiki/Patronymic "Patronymic") names are widely used instead of family names.[[31]](#cite_note-32)

##### Tamil

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Personal_name&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Tamil")]

See also: [Indian name § Tamil](/wiki/Indian_name#Tamil "Indian name")

[Tamil people](/wiki/Tamil_people "Tamil people"), generally those of younger generations, do not employ [caste](/wiki/Caste "Caste") names as surnames. This came into common use in India and also the [Tamil diaspora](/wiki/Tamil_diaspora "Tamil diaspora") in nations like Singapore after the [Dravidian movement](/wiki/Dravidian_movement "Dravidian movement") in 1930s, when the [Self respect movement](/wiki/Self_respect_movement "Self respect movement") in the 1950s and 1960s campaigned against the use of one's caste[[32]](#cite_note-33)[[33]](#cite_note-34) as part of the name. Patronymic naming system is: apart from their given name, people are described by their patronymic, that is given names (not surnames) of their father. Older generations used the initials system where the father's given name appears as an initial, for e.g.: [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language") [Hindu](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") people's names simply use initials as a prefix instead of [Patronymic suffix](/wiki/Patronymic#India "Patronymic") (father's given name) and the initials is/ are prefixed or listed first and then followed by the son's/ daughter's given name.

| Sex | Person's given name | Father's given name | Patronymic initials prefix naming system | Patronymic suffix naming system | Meaning |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Male | Rajeev | Suresh | S. Rajeev | Rajeev Suresh | Rajeev son of Suresh |
| Female | Meena | Suresh | S. Meena | Meena Suresh | Meena daughter of Suresh |

One system used for naming,[[34]](#cite_note-Karunanidhi_family-35) using only given names (without using family name or surname) is as below: for Tamil Hindu son's name using the initials[[35]](#cite_note-36) system: S. Rajeev: (initial S for father's given name Suresh and Rajeev is the son's given name). The same Tamil Hindu name using [Patronymic suffix](/wiki/Patronymic#India "Patronymic") last name system is Rajeev Suresh meaning Rajeev son of Suresh (Rajeev (first is son's given name) followed by Suresh (father's given name)). As a result, unlike surnames, while using patronymic suffix the same last name will not pass down through many generations. For Tamil Hindu daughters, the initials naming[[34]](#cite_note-Karunanidhi_family-35) system is the same, e.g.: S. Meena. Using the [Patronymic suffix](/wiki/Patronymic#India "Patronymic") system it is Meena Suresh: meaning Meena daughter of Suresh; Meena (first is daughter's given name) followed by Suresh (father's given name). As a result, unlike surnames, while using patronymic suffix the same last name will not pass down through many generations. And after marriage[[36]](#cite_note-37) the wife may or may not take her husband's given name as her last name instead of her father's. E.g.: after marriage, Meena Jagadish: meaning Meena wife of Jagadish: Meena (first is wife's given name) followed by Jagadish (husband's given name).

#### Hungary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Personal_name&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Hungary")]

Main article: [Hungarian names](/wiki/Hungarian_names "Hungarian names")

Hungary is the only European country that uses the Eastern naming order, where the given name is placed after the family name.[[37]](#cite_note-38) This usage is thought to originate from the structure of [noun phrases](/wiki/Noun_phrase "Noun phrase") in [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_language "Hungarian language") – [adjectives](/wiki/Adjective "Adjective") always precede the noun. The convention became standard in the 14th to 15th centuries, when hereditary surnames became widespread.[[38]](#cite_note-39)

[Mordvins](/wiki/Mordvins "Mordvins") use two names – a Mordvin name and a Russian name. The Mordvin name is written in the Eastern name order. Usually, the Mordvin surname is the same as the Russian surname, for example Sharononj Sandra (Russian: [Aleksandr Sharonov](/wiki/Aleksandr_Sharonov "Aleksandr Sharonov")), but it can be different at times, for example [Yovlan Olo](/wiki/Yovlan_Olo "Yovlan Olo") (Russian: Vladimir Romashkin).

## Non-human personal names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Personal_name&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Non-human personal names")]

### Animal names for each other

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Personal_name&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Animal names for each other")]

In a 2006 study published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences researchers from the University of North Carolina Wilmington studying [bottlenose dolphins](/wiki/Bottlenose_dolphin "Bottlenose dolphin") in Sarasota Bay, Florida, found that the dolphins had names for each other.[[39]](#cite_note-40) A dolphin chooses its name as an infant.[[40]](#cite_note-41)

According to a 2024 article of a study of elephant calls in [Amboseli National Park](/wiki/Amboseli_National_Park "Amboseli National Park"), Kenya and [Samburu National Reserve](/wiki/Samburu_National_Reserve "Samburu National Reserve") and [Buffalo Springs National Reserves](/wiki/Buffalo_Springs_National_Reserve "Buffalo Springs National Reserve") between 1986 and 2022 done with machine learning, elephants learn, recognize and use individualized name-like calls.[[41]](#cite_note-42)

### Names of pets

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Personal_name&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Names of pets")]

The practice of naming pets dates back at least to the 23rd century BC. An Egyptian inscription from that period mentions a dog named [Abuwtiyuw](/wiki/Abuwtiyuw "Abuwtiyuw").[[42]](#cite_note-43)

Many pet owners give human names to their pets. This has been shown to reflect the owner having a human-like relationship with the pet.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEldridge2003-44) The name given to a pet may refer to its appearance[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEldridge2003-44) or personality,[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEldridge2003-44) or be chosen for [endearment](/wiki/Endearment "Endearment"),[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEldridge2003-44) or in honor of a favorite [celebrity](/wiki/Celebrity "Celebrity").[[44]](#cite_note-45) Pet names often reflect the owner's view of the animal, and the expectations they may have for it.[[45]](#cite_note-46)[[46]](#cite_note-47)

Dog [breeders](/wiki/Animal_breeding "Animal breeding") often choose specific themes for their names, sometimes based on the number of the [litter](/wiki/Litter_(zoology) "Litter (zoology)").[[47]](#cite_note-48) In some countries, like Germany or Austria, names are chosen alphabetically, with names starting with 'A' for puppies from the first litter, 'B' for the second litter, and so on. A puppy called "Dagmar" would belong to a fourth litter. Dog owners can choose to keep the original name, or rename their pet.[[48]](#cite_note-49)

It has been argued that the giving of names to their pets allows researchers to view their pets as [ontologically](/wiki/Ontologically "Ontologically") different from unnamed [laboratory animals](/wiki/Laboratory_animal "Laboratory animal") with which they work.[[49]](#cite_note-50)
